addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- codex:codex-rescue: Proactively use when Claude Code is stuck, wants a second implementation or diagnosis pass, needs a deeper root-cause investigation, or should hand a substantial coding task to Codex through the shared runtime (Tools: Bash)

- entire-search: Search Entire checkpoint history and transcripts with `entire search --json`. Use proactively when the user asks about previous work, commits, sessions, prompts, or historical context in this repository. (Tools: Bash)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- feature-dev:code-architect: Designs feature architectures by analyzing existing codebase patterns and conventions, then providing comprehensive implementation blueprints with specific files to create/modify, component designs, data flows, and build sequences (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-explorer: Deeply analyzes existing codebase features by tracing execution paths, mapping architecture layers, understanding patterns and abstractions, and documenting dependencies to inform new development (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- feature-dev:code-reviewer: Reviews code for bugs, logic errors, security vulnerabilities, code quality issues, and adherence to project conventions, using confidence-based filtering to report only high-priority issues that truly matter (Tools: Glob, Grep, LS, Read, NotebookRead, WebFetch, TodoWrite, WebSearch, KillShell, BashOutput)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- project-structure-validator: Validates project structure against co-location and architecture patterns defined in AGENTS.md (Tools: All tools)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

- access-new-relic-logs: Use when you or the user wants to investigate logs for app.
- access-outlook-business-email: Use when you or the user wants to read, search, triage, or reason about a Microsoft 365 business mailbox, or draft a reply or new email for the user to review and send. This skill never sends — it only creates drafts.
- access-xero-for-px: Full Xero API control for the "Px Internal" Custom Connection (machine-to-machine, single org Pharmacy-Xpress). Use when you or the user wants to read or write Xero data for the PX organisation — invoices, contacts, payments, items, bank transactions, reports, payroll, files, assets. Authenticates via OAuth2 client_credentials and exposes a generic HTTP passthrough to any Xero API endpoint.
- anvil-yaml: Update Anvil app manifest files in `anvil.yaml`, including services, app metadata, and `db_schema`, using the Anvil YAML type reference available to this agent and `anvil --json validate`.
- brand-guidelines: Applies Anthropic's official brand colors and typography to any sort of artifact that may benefit from having Anthropic's look-and-feel. Use it when brand colors or style guidelines, visual formatting, or company design standards apply.
- call-another-llm: Call Codex CLI, Claude Code CLI, or Gemini CLI with a prompt and save the response to tmp/llm-responses/. Use when you need a second opinion, want to offload a subtask, or compare answers across LLMs.
- canvas-design: Create beautiful visual art in .png and .pdf documents using design philosophy. You should use this skill when the user asks to create a poster, piece of art, design, or other static piece. Create original visual designs, never copying existing artists' work to avoid copyright violations.
- chatgpt-browser-image-generation: Generate images through the user's already signed-in ChatGPT browser session, with optional brand/reference image upload. Use whenever the user asks an agent to "make an image with ChatGPT", "use ChatGPT to generate <N> images", produce branded artwork, mock-ups, icons, hero illustrations, or social assets through their ChatGPT subscription instead of an image API. Open one tab per distinct request and run them in parallel; if requests are variations of the same content, keep them in a single chat. Downloaded images land in the user's Downloads folder and the script moves them into the working directory.
- chatgpt-pro-browser-query: Query ChatGPT through the user's already signed-in browser session while proving ChatGPT Pro and Extended Pro are active before sending. Use this whenever the user asks an agent to ask ChatGPT, use ChatGPT Pro, compare with ChatGPT, query the ChatGPT web app, or get a second opinion specifically from their ChatGPT subscription instead of the API.
- chrome-control: Shared Chrome DevTools control bridge that owns the single CDP connection so multiple Chrome-using skills run from one Chrome "Allow control" approval. Use as STEP 1 of any skill that drives the user's Chrome (ChatGPT browser query, ChatGPT image generation, future browser-driving skills).
- chrome-devtools-cli: Use this skill to write shell scripts or run shell commands to automate tasks in the browser or otherwise use Chrome DevTools via CLI.
- claude-epic-pr-review: Token-intensive, maximal review of a large pull request or branch. Runs three parallel review streams — a 5-agent Codex xhigh swarm with overlapping coverage, an adversarial multi-agent epic-review workflow, and a fork that validates every existing PR comment after checks pass — then consolidates all findings, auto-fixes valid code bugs in parallel while asking the user about UX / DB-schema / new-file decisions, runs /code-review max + /simplify max, and replaces the PR. Use for large, high-risk, or cross-cutting changes when an exhaustive, deliberately expensive review is wanted.
- claude-team-review-plan-implementation: Launch a large team of agents (Codex, Gemini, Opus teammates) to line-by-line compare a reference plan against its implementation across multiple repos, extract deviations, validate, fix, verify, triage design decisions with the user, simplify, and create PRs. Accepts a reference file name as its argument.
- client-forms: Create, inspect, edit, style, validate, and convert Anvil client Forms under `client_code`, including Form Python, HTML templates, event wiring, Data Bindings, RepeatingPanel item templates, reusable Forms, theme CSS, and legacy YAML templates.
- client-modules: Edit Anvil client code Python modules under `client_code` that are not form definitions, without touching read-only dependencies or form templates.
- data-models: Design idiomatic Anvil data models and client/server data flow for new apps, CRUD workflows, Data Tables, model classes, buffering, drafts, Data Bindings, and server methods. Use before choosing an architecture for persisted domain data, when the user asks about Anvil models/model classes, or when deciding whether to pass rows, model objects, dicts, or server-callable payloads.
- doc-coauthoring: Guide users through a structured workflow for co-authoring documentation. Use when user wants to write documentation, proposals, technical specs, decision docs, or similar structured content. This workflow helps users efficiently transfer context, refine content through iteration, and verify the doc works for readers. Trigger when user mentions writing docs, creating proposals, drafting specs, or similar documentation tasks.
- docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- heavy-swarm-plan: Build a read-only implementation plan for complex, multi-area work and pressure-test that plan with Codex CLI, Claude Code CLI, and Gemini CLI before implementation starts. Use when the task is large, ambiguous, cross-cutting, or risky enough that a normal quick plan is not sufficient.
- implement-good-test-patterns: Use datasette-enrichments as a testing reference and imitate its pytest patterns when adding or restructuring tests. Use when writing tests, improving test structure, or aligning a Python project with good integration-style pytest patterns.
- mailshot-carehomes: Use when you or the user wants to send a single bulk announcement (mailshot) to every care-home portal contact - e.g. a new branding link, a portal change, a service notice. Gathers all care-home recipient emails from the Anvil carehome_portal_users table and the Neon "PX" script-copy table, then drafts ONE email (FROM team@, CC stacey@, BCC every care-home contact + zoe@/paresh@/mandeep@) via the access-outlook-business-email skill. Never sends - it only creates a draft for the user to review and send.
- manage-m365-tenant: Use when you or the user wants to administer the Microsoft 365 tenancy — users, groups, licenses, roles, domains, devices, policies, security alerts, audit logs, SharePoint sites, Teams, and usage reports. Full read/write control via Microsoft Graph.
- mk-feature-plan-mode: Plan-only feature design mode. The user states a feature objective; read-only forks map the existing codebase (data model, screens, modules) while the main agent grills the user via AskUser to lock down every UX edge case and scenario. The locked plan is then reviewed by ~2 parallel Codex agents plus an architecture-review sub-agent; any new gaps loop back to the user. Once locked, it writes a feature_plan_<feature>.html design doc to the cwd (UI screens, schema changes, file-tree of new/changed files, out-of-scope) and opens it in the user's default browser, plus a migration.py (test branch by default, --live for prod) only when the schema changes are not purely additive. Use when the user says "feature plan mode", "plan this feature", "design doc for ...", or wants a reviewable UX/architecture plan before any code is written.
- pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- performance-metrics-from-new-relic: Use when you or the user wants frontend performance metrics from New Relic, including Largest Contentful Paint, Interaction to Next Paint, Cumulative Layout Shift, poor distributions, and worst URLs or sessions.
- pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates, layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- pr-swarm-review: Fetch comments from an open GitHub pull request, then use a single sub-agent to extract every reviewer's substantive concerns and validate each one against the codebase in one pass, and consolidate the verdicts back in the main agent for triage and fixing. Use when the user wants existing PR comments triaged before deciding what to fix.
- quick-smoke-tests: Generate an ordered, dependency-aware list of UI smoke tests for the work just completed in this session. Walk the user through every test using the AskUser tool — one question per test with all details embedded in the question — then validate each reported result against the database. Use when the user says "smoke test what we just did", "let's quickly verify the changes in the UI", or similar after implementation work.
- server-code: Edit Anvil server code Python modules under `server_code`, including server-callable functions, background logic, and server packages, while keeping changes isolated to the app workspace.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- sync-global-user-instructions-md: Sync one canonical global instruction file into user-global Codex, Claude, and Gemini instruction files. Use when you need to update the canonical user-global instructions, diff installed global files against canon, reconcile drift, or replace user-global AGENTS/CLAUDE/GEMINI files from the canonical source.
- sync-mcps: Sync canonical MCP server manifests into Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, and Cursor config files. Use when you need to add, remove, or update an MCP across all agents from one source of truth, or to diff installed MCP configs against canon.
- theme-factory: Toolkit for styling artifacts with a theme. These artifacts can be slides, docs, reportings, HTML landing pages, etc. There are 10 pre-set themes with colors/fonts that you can apply to any artifact that has been creating, or can generate a new theme on-the-fly.
- webapp-testing: Toolkit for interacting with and testing local web applications using Playwright. Supports verifying frontend functionality, debugging UI behavior, capturing browser screenshots, and viewing browser logs.
- windows-computer-use: Drive the Windows desktop UI the way a user can — list monitors/windows, read UIA element trees, click/right-click/double-click, type text, send key chords, scroll, drag, and capture screenshots across multiple monitors and any app type. Use when a task needs native Windows app or OS control beyond the browser (File Explorer, Settings, installers, system dialogs, Office desktop, WinUI/UWP/Win32 apps). For controlling a web browser, prefer chrome-control / the browser skills instead.
- xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- feature-dev:feature-dev: Guided feature development with codebase understanding and architecture focus
- astral:ruff: Guide for using ruff, the extremely fast Python linter and formatter. Use this when linting, formatting, or fixing Python code.
- astral:ty: Guide for using ty, the extremely fast Python type checker and language server. Use this when type checking Python code or setting up type checking in Python projects.
- astral:uv: Guide for using uv, the Python package and project manager. Use this when working with Python projects, scripts, packages, or tools.
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- understand-anything:understand: Analyze a codebase to produce an interactive knowledge graph for understanding architecture, components, and relationships
- understand-anything:understand-chat: Use when you need to ask questions about a codebase or understand code using a knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-dashboard: Launch the interactive web dashboard to visualize a codebase's knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-diff: Use when you need to analyze git diffs or pull requests to understand what changed, affected components, and risks
- understand-anything:understand-domain: Extract business domain knowledge from a codebase and generate an interactive domain flow graph. Works standalone (lightweight scan) or derives from an existing /understand knowledge graph.
- understand-anything:understand-explain: Use when you need a deep-dive explanation of a specific file, function, or module in the codebase
- understand-anything:understand-knowledge: Analyze a Karpathy-pattern LLM wiki knowledge base and generate an interactive knowledge graph with entity extraction, implicit relationships, and topic clustering.
- understand-anything:understand-onboard: Use when you need to generate an onboarding guide for new team members joining a project
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud (requires claude.ai account access)). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	name: Nightly upstream merge
2	
3	# The ongoing half of the architecture. Nightly: if upstream superset published a
4	# newer desktop-v* tag than .fork/upstream-baseline.txt, merge that tag into the
5	# fork. git's 3-way merge handles the non-conflicting majority deterministically;
6	# Opus (--effort high) resolves ONLY the conflicted files, preserving every
7	# feature. Then the FEATURES.md marker gate runs, then the deterministic ARM64
8	# build. On a clean merge + green build the build publishes a full Release and we
9	# fast-forward the baseline. Any failure (unresolvable conflict, lost marker,
10	# build failure) hard-aborts and leaves the baseline untouched -> recovery loop.
11	#
12	# AI cost scales with the UPSTREAM delta per release, NOT with how large the fork
13	# grows. The build itself stays 100% AI-free.
14	#
15	# NOTE: schedule only fires from the DEFAULT branch, so this is dormant until the
16	# fork-baseline branch is promoted to main.
17	
18	on:
19	  schedule:
20	    - cron: '0 5 * * *' # 05:00 UTC daily
21	  workflow_dispatch:
22	    inputs:
23	      force_tag:
24	        description: 'Merge a specific upstream desktop-v* tag (blank = latest)'
25	        required: false
26	        default: ''
27	
28	permissions:
29	  contents: write
30	
31	jobs:
32	  merge:
33	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
34	    outputs:
35	      should_build: ${{ steps.do.outputs.should_build }}
36	      newtag: ${{ steps.do.outputs.newtag }}
37	      version: ${{ steps.do.outputs.version }}
38	      candidate: ${{ steps.do.outputs.candidate }}
39	    env:
40	      CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
41	      DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS: '1'
42	      CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC: '1'
43	      # Conflict resolution can touch large files; give Opus headroom.
44	      CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS: '128000'
45	    steps:
46	      - name: Checkout fork (full history)
47	        uses: actions/checkout@v5
48	        with:
49	          fetch-depth: 0
50	
51	      - name: Setup Node.js
52	        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
53	        with:
54	          node-version: '22'
55	
56	      # (LOCK-REGEN) Lock regeneration below needs bun; pin the EXACT version
57	      # the build uses so the lock we commit is byte-identical to what the
58	      # frozen build install consumes.
59	      - name: Setup Bun (match the build's pinned version)
60	        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
61	        with:
62	          bun-version: '1.3.14'
63	
64	      - name: Merge upstream + resolve + gate
65	        id: do
66	        shell: bash
67	        run: |
68	          set -euo pipefail
69	          git config user.name "superset-arm64-bot"
70	          git config user.email "actions@users.noreply.github.com"
71	
72	          # --- detect the upstream tag to merge --------------------------------
73	          git remote add upstream https://github.com/superset-sh/superset.git 2>/dev/null || true
74	          # Fetch upstream's desktop-v* tags into an ISOLATED ref namespace.
75	          # Our OWN one-version release tags live in refs/tags/desktop-v<version>
76	          # (gh release create makes them at our build commit). A plain fetch into
77	          # refs/tags/ collides with every released version ("would clobber existing
78	          # tag" -> non-zero fetch -> set -e abort). The isolated namespace + --force
79	          # keeps upstream's source tags cleanly separate from our release tags.
80	          git fetch --no-recurse-submodules --force upstream \
81	            'refs/tags/desktop-v*:refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*'
82	          FORCE='${{ github.event.inputs.force_tag }}'
83	          if [ -n "$FORCE" ]; then
84	            NEWTAG="$FORCE"
85	          else
86	            LATEST=$(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:lstrip=2)' 'refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*' | sed -n 's/^desktop-v\([0-9][0-9.]*\)$/\1/p' | sort -V | tail -1)
87	            NEWTAG="desktop-v${LATEST}"
88	          fi
89	          BASE=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .fork/upstream-baseline.txt)
90	          echo "baseline=$BASE  latest_upstream=$NEWTAG"
91	          if [ "$NEWTAG" = "$BASE" ]; then
92	            echo "Already at $BASE — nothing to merge."
93	            echo "should_build=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
94	            exit 0

file.content:
name: Nightly upstream merge

# The ongoing half of the architecture. Nightly: if upstream superset published a
# newer desktop-v* tag than .fork/upstream-baseline.txt, merge that tag into the
# fork. git's 3-way merge handles the non-conflicting majority deterministically;
# Opus (--effort high) resolves ONLY the conflicted files, preserving every
# feature. Then the FEATURES.md marker gate runs, then the deterministic ARM64
# build. On a clean merge + green build the build publishes a full Release and we
# fast-forward the baseline. Any failure (unresolvable conflict, lost marker,
# build failure) hard-aborts and leaves the baseline untouched -> recovery loop.
#
# AI cost scales with the UPSTREAM delta per release, NOT with how large the fork
# grows. The build itself stays 100% AI-free.
#
# NOTE: schedule only fires from the DEFAULT branch, so this is dormant until the
# fork-baseline branch is promoted to main.

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 5 * * *' # 05:00 UTC daily
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      force_tag:
        description: 'Merge a specific upstream desktop-v* tag (blank = latest)'
        required: false
        default: ''

permissions:
  contents: write

jobs:
  merge:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      should_build: ${{ steps.do.outputs.should_build }}
      newtag: ${{ steps.do.outputs.newtag }}
      version: ${{ steps.do.outputs.version }}
      candidate: ${{ steps.do.outputs.candidate }}
    env:
      CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
      DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS: '1'
      CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC: '1'
      # Conflict resolution can touch large files; give Opus headroom.
      CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS: '128000'
    steps:
      - name: Checkout fork (full history)
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Setup Node.js
        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '22'

      # (LOCK-REGEN) Lock regeneration below needs bun; pin the EXACT version
      # the build uses so the lock we commit is byte-identical to what the
      # frozen build install consumes.
      - name: Setup Bun (match the build's pinned version)
        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
        with:
          bun-version: '1.3.14'

      - name: Merge upstream + resolve + gate
        id: do
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          git config user.name "superset-arm64-bot"
          git config user.email "actions@users.noreply.github.com"

          # --- detect the upstream tag to merge --------------------------------
          git remote add upstream https://github.com/superset-sh/superset.git 2>/dev/null || true
          # Fetch upstream's desktop-v* tags into an ISOLATED ref namespace.
          # Our OWN one-version release tags live in refs/tags/desktop-v<version>
          # (gh release create makes them at our build commit). A plain fetch into
          # refs/tags/ collides with every released version ("would clobber existing
          # tag" -> non-zero fetch -> set -e abort). The isolated namespace + --force
          # keeps upstream's source tags cleanly separate from our release tags.
          git fetch --no-recurse-submodules --force upstream \
            'refs/tags/desktop-v*:refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*'
          FORCE='${{ github.event.inputs.force_tag }}'
          if [ -n "$FORCE" ]; then
            NEWTAG="$FORCE"
          else
            LATEST=$(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:lstrip=2)' 'refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*' | sed -n 's/^desktop-v\([0-9][0-9.]*\)$/\1/p' | sort -V | tail -1)
            NEWTAG="desktop-v${LATEST}"
          fi
          BASE=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .fork/upstream-baseline.txt)
          echo "baseline=$BASE  latest_upstream=$NEWTAG"
          if [ "$NEWTAG" = "$BASE" ]; then
            echo "Already at $BASE — nothing to merge."
            echo "should_build=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
            exit 0
          fi
          if ! git rev-parse -q --verify "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}" >/dev/null; then
            echo "::error::upstream tag $NEWTAG not found"; exit 1
          fi

          # --- candidate branch + merge ---------------------------------------
          CAND="nightly/${NEWTAG}"
          git switch -c "$CAND"
          echo "Merging $NEWTAG into the fork (no auto-commit)…"
          set +e
          git merge --no-commit --no-ff "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}"
          set -e
          CONFLICTS=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
          if [ -n "$CONFLICTS" ]; then
            echo "::group::conflicts to resolve"; echo "$CONFLICTS"; echo "::endgroup::"
            npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
            PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are inside a git repository mid-merge: upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} is being merged into our Windows-ARM64 fork. ONLY the files listed by 'git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U' are conflicted. Resolve EVERY conflicted file: combine upstream's change with our fork's feature so BOTH intents survive and our feature keeps working; where upstream deliberately removed or refactored something we had only lightly touched, follow upstream. Do NOT edit any non-conflicted file. Do NOT weaken or remove any feature marker tracked in FEATURES.md. After resolving each file run 'git add <file>'. Do NOT commit. Do NOT run any other git command. When every conflict is resolved and staged, stop. Output nothing."
            attempt=1
            while [ $attempt -le 3 ]; do
              echo "::group::claude resolve attempt $attempt"
              claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus --effort high -p "$PROMPT" || true
              echo "::endgroup::"
              if [ -z "$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)" ]; then break; fi
              attempt=$((attempt+1))
            done
            REMAIN=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
            if [ -n "$REMAIN" ]; then
              echo "::error::Opus could not resolve all conflicts for $NEWTAG — recovery: fix locally and re-baseline. Remaining:"; echo "$REMAIN"
              exit 1
            fi
          else
            echo "Clean merge — no conflicts."
          fi

          # --- feature-marker gate (FEATURES.md) ------------------------------
          echo "::group::feature-marker gate"
          MISSING=0
          # read the fenced ```markers block: lines are "<token><TAB><glob-root>"
          awk '/^```markers$/{f=1;next} /^```$/{f=0} f' FEATURES.md | while IFS=$'\t' read -r tok root; do
            [ -n "$tok" ] || continue
            if grep -rqsF -- "$tok" "$root" 2>/dev/null; then
              echo "  OK   $tok ($root)"
            else
              echo "::error::feature marker MISSING after merge: '$tok' under $root"
              echo missing >> "$RUNNER_TEMP/marker-missing"
            fi
          done
          echo "::endgroup::"
          if [ -f "$RUNNER_TEMP/marker-missing" ]; then
            echo "::error::A feature was dropped by the $NEWTAG merge — rejecting. Recovery: fix locally and re-baseline."
            exit 1
          fi

          # --- dependency-consistency gate + lock regen (LOCK-REGEN) ----------
          # The build installs --frozen-lockfile and electron-builder's
          # dependency traversal requires each workspace's DECLARED dep version
          # to be the one actually installed. A merge introduces two kinds of
          # drift that both kill the build ~25 min in (after a windows-arm64
          # runner spins up), not here:
          #   (1) a fork root `overrides` entry pins a package to a version
          #       DIFFERENT from the merged workspace pin (this is exactly how a
          #       stale {@mastra/core, mastracode} override broke every mastra
          #       bump: override forced 0.18.0, chat declared 0.18.1 ->
          #       electron-builder "production dependency not found 0.18.1"); and
          #   (2) the merge bumps a package.json dep but bun.lock still pins the
          #       old version (a stale frozen lock).
          # Catch (1) loud here (pure manifest analysis, platform-independent);
          # fix (2) by regenerating the lock with the build's exact bun version,
          # which ALSO fails loud if the merged manifests don't resolve or a pin
          # is age-blocked. Both run in this 2-min job, so the windows-arm64
          # build never spins up on a broken tree. We deliberately do NOT run a
          # full `--frozen-lockfile` install here: this is a linux runner, so a
          # platform-specific install/native-build quirk would be a false abort,
          # and after a successful --lockfile-only the lock is consistent with
          # the manifests by construction (same bun version the build uses).
          # Any failure below hard-aborts with the baseline untouched.
          echo "::group::dependency-consistency gate"
          node scripts/check-override-consistency.mjs
          echo "::endgroup::"

          echo "::group::regenerate bun.lock to match merged manifests"
          export SUPERSET_POSTINSTALL_RUNNING=1
          LOCKLOG="$RUNNER_TEMP/bun-lockfile.log"
          set +e
          bun install --lockfile-only > "$LOCKLOG" 2>&1
          rc=$?
          set -e
          cat "$LOCKLOG"
          if [ "$rc" -ne 0 ]; then
            if grep -q "blocked by minimum-release-age" "$LOCKLOG"; then
              echo "::error::Lock regen blocked by bunfig minimumReleaseAge — an upstream dep pin in $NEWTAG is <72h old. This is a timing race, not a breakage: the nightly will succeed once the version ages past the gate (re-run after it ages, or wait for tomorrow's schedule). Baseline left untouched."
            else
              echo "::error::bun install --lockfile-only failed (rc=$rc) after merging $NEWTAG — the merged manifests don't resolve. Recovery: fix locally and re-baseline."
            fi
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "::endgroup::"

          # --- record new baseline + commit the merge -------------------------
          printf '%s\n' "$NEWTAG" > .fork/upstream-baseline.txt
          git add -A
          git commit -m "merge upstream ${NEWTAG}" -m "Auto-merged by nightly-merge; conflicts resolved by Opus where present; all FEATURES.md markers verified present."
          # (CANDIDATE-IDEMPOTENT-PUSH) The candidate is a disposable CI branch:
          # re-derived from main each run, consumed by `build`, deleted by
          # `advance` ON SUCCESS only. A FAILED build never reaches `advance`, so
          # it strands the candidate on the remote. The next night re-merges the
          # SAME upstream version into a NEW commit (AI conflict resolution is
          # non-deterministic AND main may have advanced), whose history diverges
          # from the stale branch — a plain push is then rejected non-fast-forward
          # and EVERY subsequent nightly deadlocks on that stale ref. Drop any
          # stale remote copy first so the push is always clean. This is pure CI
          # scratch (never a feature/history branch), so overwriting it is safe;
          # we delete-then-push rather than --force a real branch.
          git push origin --delete "$CAND" 2>/dev/null || true
          git push origin "$CAND"

          VERSION=$(node -p "require('./apps/desktop/package.json').version")
          echo "should_build=true" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "newtag=$NEWTAG" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "version=$VERSION" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "candidate=$CAND" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
          echo "Merged $NEWTAG -> candidate $CAND (version $VERSION). Building next."

  build:
    needs: merge
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    uses: ./.github/workflows/build-arm64.yml
    with:
      ref: ${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}
      publish: true

  advance:
    needs: [merge, build]
    if: needs.merge.outputs.should_build == 'true'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout
        uses: actions/checkout@v5
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Fast-forward main to the verified candidate + clean up
        shell: bash
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          CAND='${{ needs.merge.outputs.candidate }}'
          git fetch origin "$CAND"
          # The candidate is main + the merge commit, so this is a fast-forward.
          git push origin FETCH_HEAD:refs/heads/main
          git push origin --delete "$CAND"
          echo "Baseline advanced to ${{ needs.merge.outputs.newtag }}; Release published by the build job."

content.content:
name: Deploy Relay

# The relay ships to Fly (not Vercel), so it's not part of deploy-production.yml.
# Auto-deploys only when relay-relevant code changes — a 7-region rolling Fly
# deploy is too heavy to run on every unrelated push to main. Use the manual
# "Run workflow" button (workflow_dispatch) to force a deploy, e.g. to ship a
# relay change that merged before this workflow existed.
on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - "apps/relay/**"
      - "packages/shared/**"
      - ".github/workflows/deploy-relay.yml"
  workflow_dispatch:

# Never let two relay deploys overlap — rolling deploys mutate the live fleet.
concurrency:
  group: deploy-relay
  cancel-in-progress: false

jobs:
  deploy-relay:
    name: Deploy Relay to Fly
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    environment: production

    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4.3.1
        with:
          # The deploy script only reads the working tree; no git auth needed.
          persist-credentials: false

      - name: Install flyctl
        run: |
          curl -L https://fly.io/install.sh | sh
          echo "$HOME/.fly/bin" >> "$GITHUB_PATH"

      - name: Deploy relay (scale + rolling deploy + smoke test)

59:| ReferenceError-class build gate | the build (and therefore every nightly, whose build job calls the same workflow) fails on cannot-find-name tsc diagnostics ONLY (TS2304/2552/2662/2663/18004) — bare identifiers esbuild bundles fine but that throw ReferenceError at runtime, exactly what a CLEAN-merging upstream hunk can leave in fork code the AI resolver never sees (v1.14.0 layout.tsx incident: upstream renamed the workspace source variable in a non-conflicting hunk; fork's cache-first hold kept referencing it; every v2 workspace view crashed). Per-package direct tsc (turbo graph is debt-blocked); the accepted type debt has zero of these codes so the gate can't false-abort | `(REFERR-GATE)` |
60:| Nightly dependency-consistency + lock regen | the nightly merge can introduce dependency-state drift that only the build discovers (after a windows-arm64 runner spins up): a stale fork root `overrides` entry that pins a package to a version different from the merged workspace pin (electron-builder "production dependency not found …"), or a `bun.lock` left stale by an upstream package.json bump (frozen-install fails). The merge now runs an override-consistency gate (fails loud on any exact override↔pin mismatch) + regenerates `bun.lock` to match the merged manifests with the build's exact bun version (which also fails loud, in the cheap merge job, if the merged manifests don't resolve or a pin is age-blocked) — so this class of build failure is caught/fixed before the windows-arm64 build spins up, baseline untouched on failure (recovery loop). No full `--frozen-lockfile` install runs here: the merge runner is linux, so a platform-specific install quirk would be a false abort, and the regenerated lock is consistent with the manifests by construction. The fork carries NO mastra `overrides` and pins `@mastra/core`/`mastracode` to upstream's exact versions in all three workspaces (upstream keeps mastra consistent via identical exact pins; the seed commit's redundant override + a recovery merge that resolved the pins DOWN to 0.18.0 were the recurring breakage, both corrected) | `(LOCK-REGEN)` |

# Fork feature manifest

This file is the source of truth for **what our fork adds on top of upstream
superset**. The nightly upstream-merge workflow greps every marker below against
the merged tree; **if any marker is missing the merge is rejected** (a feature
was dropped or an upstream change clobbered it) and the build hard-aborts into
the recovery loop. Markers are deliberately distinctive identifiers that only
exist because of our feature code.

When you add a new custom feature, add a marker row here in the same commit.
When upstream **deprecates/removes** a feature we mirrored, remove its marker row
in the merge that drops it (the only legitimate way a marker leaves this list).

## Features

| Feature | What it is | Marker |
|---|---|---|
| Native Windows ARM64 packaging | one-click NSIS installer; ARM64 node-pty/libsql/tokenizers | `npm:@lydell/node-pty`, `bun-windows-arm64` |
| Window controls | native `titleBarOverlay` is the sole min/max/close set on Windows | `titleBarOverlay` |
| Windows behaviour fixes | child-process patch, cmd.exe fallback, force-foreground, watchdog, WebGL recovery, fast startup | `windows-child-process` |
| Agent status dots (Claude + Codex) | per-terminal dot + workspace rollup; host-service POST + JSONL watcher | `pane-map-hook`, `StatusIndicator` |
| Claude Stop unhooked from notify.sh | upstream's raw Stop passthrough must never race superset-notify.py (it wiped the blue dot + yellow-hold) | `CLAUDE-STOP-UNHOOKED` |
| Compaction shows working | Claude context compaction (manual /compact + auto) drives the yellow dot via PreCompact / SessionStart(source=compact) | `(COMPACT-YELLOW)` |
| Team/workflow work shows working | background_tasks[] entry types split the post-turn dot: agent-type work (teammate/subagent/workflow) holds yellow; shell-only goes blue | `(TEAM-YELLOW)` |
| Codex-companion job holds the dot | detached codex worker is invisible to background_tasks[]; an active (pid-alive) codex job for the session holds yellow incl. through StopFailure AND the JSONL watcher's stream-idle/API-abort + interrupt self-heal (System 1 mirrors the same check via `codexJobActive` in agent-jsonl-watcher.ts, so a codex-only session can't false-green when the Claude API drops and no StopFailure hook fires) | `_codex_job_active` |
| Manual compact restores shell-blue | a manual /compact ending while only a background shell runs restores the BackgroundRunning blue (turn-end snapshot marker) instead of false-greening; the JSONL-watcher re-attach after compaction must not wipe that blue | `_shellbg_marker_path`, `(BLUE-SPECTATOR)` |
| ws native modules off | host-service `ws` must use pure-JS mask/unmask — a broken packaged bufferutil wedges WS receivers and kills ALL terminal keyboard input | `WS_NO_BUFFER_UTIL` |
| Shell-running blue dot | OSC 133 C/D command-running detection | `scanForOsc133Cd` |
| Non-git / multi-repo workspaces | open a non-git folder as a plain workspace | `resolveNonGitFolder` |
| Multi-repo branch workspaces | "Open from multi-folder" groups N git repos; "+" fans the same branch out as a worktree per repo under one container workspace | `readMultiRepoConfig`, `createMultiRepoWorkspaceFlow` |

| Auto-resume stuck chats | when a Claude chat dies on an API failure the JSONL watcher classifies it (deny-list-first: rate-limit-with-reset-time / transient-rate-limit / server-error / connection-drop / half-stop are resumable; auth/policy/bad-image/model-unavailable are notify-only) after a finality/quiescence gate, and a durable MAIN-side registry auto-sends "resume from exactly where everything was left" — fire-at-parsed-reset-time (+30s, DST-correct, fire-now-if-already-past, ≤8d sane cap) for usage limits, else a 60s→×3 backoff capped at 5 sends / 24h. Every send is gated at fire time (toggle on, still the last meaningful transcript line, host-service `commandRunning`=idle + agent-session binding match) so it can never auto-confirm a menu, land mid-turn, or run at a bare shell. Any trusted interaction with that terminal cancels (persisted tombstone survives reload/restart). Account-wide cohorts are jittered + concurrency-capped. Global toggle (default on). Claude-only in v1 — Codex auto-resume is deferred (the on-disk corpus has no validated standalone usage-limit signal). Dots untouched (separate IPC variant + toasts) | `(AUTO-RESUME)`, `autoResumeManager`, `writeInputIfIdleSession`, `useAutoResumeActivity`, `AutoResumeController` |
| Dot-decision log + codex pid-reuse cap | superset-notify.py writes an always-on bounded `~/.superset/logs/dot-decisions.log` line at each terminal turn-end decision (the POSTed eventType + WHY a hold won — codex job / background_tasks / subagent markers / GREEN / BLUE / RED), and `_codex_job_active` age-caps pid-bearing `running` codex job records at 6h so a recycled PID can't pin the dot yellow forever (a cap-skipped stale record is logged, so a premature-green is auditable) | `_decision_log` |
| Idle/zombie teammate hold reaped | an agent set Claude Code still reports `running` in background_tasks[] but that has produced NO activity (SubagentStart / subagent-scoped PostToolUse refresh a `.bgactive` timer) for 15 min stops pinning the lead's dot yellow — the harness never flips an idle long-lived teammate (or one that died without a clean SubagentStop) to a finished status, so the hold previously lasted until the team was disbanded / the session ended. The JSONL watcher re-asserts yellow if a reaped set is in fact still writing | `(BG-STALE)` |
| Idle teammates green deterministically | Claude Code reports finished teammates in the Stop payload's background_tasks[] as `status:"running"` FOREVER (captured live: 33 teammate entries, all "running", for a session whose 44 teammates had all finished hours earlier) — and the (BG-STALE) reap only runs inside hook events, so a session whose LAST event landed while the set was <15 min idle latched yellow indefinitely (nothing ever re-evaluated; 9 terminals observed stuck). superset-notify.py now keeps a per-terminal teammate LEDGER parsed incrementally from the lead session transcript (payload `transcript_path`; offset+state cached in `<terminalId>.teamstate.json`, keyed by transcript path so /resume self-invalidates): Agent-spawn / SendMessage-wake / non-idle teammate- or agent-messages mark a name active, an `idle_notification` teammate-message marks it idle. At Stop/SubagentStop, when EVERY tracked teammate is provably idle, unfinished teammate-type entries are dropped BEFORE the split/snapshot/hold decision — the lead greens (or blues on a shell remainder) at its actual final turn end instead of holding forever. Positive proof only: no transcript / no teammates seen / any parse error / any teammate not idle → entries kept (old behaviour + (BG-STALE) unchanged behind it); raw fork agentIds ("a"+hex) are excluded from the predicate (forks have real hook lifecycles and would never idle-notify); subagent/workflow/shell entries always pass through untouched; bg_ids marker reconciliation still uses the RAW list. Decision log carries a `[teammate-idle: dropped N ...]` suffix for auditability | `(TEAMMATE-IDLE)` |
| ReferenceError-class build gate | the build (and therefore every nightly, whose build job calls the same workflow) fails on cannot-find-name tsc diagnostics ONLY (TS2304/2552/2662/2663/18004) — bare identifiers esbuild bundles fine but that throw ReferenceError at runtime, exactly what a CLEAN-merging upstream hunk can leave in fork code the AI resolver never sees (v1.14.0 layout.tsx incident: upstream renamed the workspace source variable in a non-conflicting hunk; fork's cache-first hold kept referencing it; every v2 workspace view crashed). Per-package direct tsc (turbo graph is debt-blocked); the accepted type debt has zero of these codes so the gate can't false-abort | `(REFERR-GATE)` |
| Nightly dependency-consistency + lock regen | the nightly merge can introduce dependency-state drift that only the build discovers (after a windows-arm64 runner spins up): a stale fork root `overrides` entry that pins a package to a version different from the merged workspace pin (electron-builder "production dependency not found …"), or a `bun.lock` left stale by an upstream package.json bump (frozen-install fails). The merge now runs an override-consistency gate (fails loud on any exact override↔pin mismatch) + regenerates `bun.lock` to match the merged manifests with the build's exact bun version (which also fails loud, in the cheap merge job, if the merged manifests don't resolve or a pin is age-blocked) — so this class of build failure is caught/fixed before the windows-arm64 build spins up, baseline untouched on failure (recovery loop). No full `--frozen-lockfile` install runs here: the merge runner is linux, so a platform-specific install quirk would be a false abort, and the regenerated lock is consistent with the manifests by construction. The fork carries NO mastra `overrides` and pins `@mastra/core`/`mastracode` to upstream's exact versions in all three workspaces (upstream keeps mastra consistent via identical exact pins; the seed commit's redundant override + a recovery merge that resolved the pins DOWN to 0.18.0 were the recurring breakage, both corrected) | `(LOCK-REGEN)` |

  errors). Marker `(HOOK-FORK-DIET)`; payload is byte-identical to the pipelines.

content.text:
`low effort → 1 diff pass → no verify → ≤4 findings`

## Turn 1 — read

One tool call: read the unified diff (`git diff @{upstream}...HEAD; git diff HEAD`
to cover both committed and uncommitted changes, or `git diff main...HEAD` /
the target passed as an argument). Skip test/fixture
hunks (`test/`, `spec/`, `__tests__/`, `*_test.*`, `*.test.*`,
`fixtures/`, `testdata/`) — test-file changes are not reviewed at this level.
No subagents, no full-file reads.

## Turn 2 — findings

Flag runtime-correctness bugs visible from the hunk alone: inverted/wrong
condition, off-by-one, null/undefined deref where adjacent lines show the value
can be absent, removed guard, falsy-zero check, missing `await`,
wrong-variable copy-paste, error swallowed in a catch that should propagate.
Also flag — still from the hunk alone — new code that duplicates an existing
helper visible in the diff context, and dead code the diff leaves behind.

Do **not** flag style, naming, perf, missing tests, or anything outside the
hunk.

Output at most **4 findings**, most-severe first, one line each:
`path/to/file.ext:123 — what's wrong and the concrete failure`. If nothing
qualifies, output exactly `(none)`.

Positive proof only: no transcript / no teammates seen / any parse error / any teammate not idle → entries kept (old behaviour + (BG-STALE) unchanged behind it); raw fork agentIds ("a"+hex) are excluded from the predicate (forks have real hook lifecycles and would never idle-notify); subagent/workflow/shell entries always pass through untouched; bg_ids marker reconciliation still uses the RAW list. 1	name: Nightly upstream merge
2	
3	# The ongoing half of the architecture. Nightly: if upstream superset published a
4	# newer desktop-v* tag than .fork/upstream-baseline.txt, merge that tag into the
5	# fork. git's 3-way merge handles the non-conflicting majority deterministically;
6	# Opus (--effort high) resolves ONLY the conflicted files, preserving every
7	# feature. Then the FEATURES.md marker gate runs, then the dependency gates, then
8	# (MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE) an Opus review of the WHOLE merged delta — clean or
9	# conflicted — against the fork feature manifest (a cleanly-merging upstream hunk
10	# can semantically break a fork feature with zero conflicts; v1.14.2 dropped the
11	# sidebar snooze/bin fields that way). Only then the deterministic ARM64 build.
12	# On a clean merge + green build the build publishes a full Release and we
13	# fast-forward the baseline. Any failure (unresolvable conflict, lost marker,
14	# semantic breakage verdict, build failure) hard-aborts and leaves the baseline
15	# untouched -> recovery loop.
16	#
17	# AI cost scales with the UPSTREAM delta per release, NOT with how large the fork
18	# grows. The build itself stays 100% AI-free.
19	#
20	# NOTE: schedule only fires from the DEFAULT branch, so this is dormant until the
21	# fork-baseline branch is promoted to main.
22	
23	on:
24	  schedule:
25	    - cron: '0 5 * * *' # 05:00 UTC daily
26	  workflow_dispatch:
27	    inputs:
28	      force_tag:
29	        description: 'Merge a specific upstream desktop-v* tag (blank = latest)'
30	        required: false
31	        default: ''
32	
33	permissions:
34	  contents: write
35	
36	jobs:
37	  merge:
38	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
39	    outputs:
40	      should_build: ${{ steps.do.outputs.should_build }}
41	      newtag: ${{ steps.do.outputs.newtag }}
42	      version: ${{ steps.do.outputs.version }}
43	      candidate: ${{ steps.do.outputs.candidate }}
44	    env:
45	      CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN }}
46	      DISABLE_NON_ESSENTIAL_MODEL_CALLS: '1'
47	      CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC: '1'
48	      # Conflict resolution can touch large files; give Opus headroom.
49	      CLAUDE_CODE_MAX_OUTPUT_TOKENS: '128000'
50	    steps:
51	      - name: Checkout fork (full history)
52	        uses: actions/checkout@v5
53	        with:
54	          fetch-depth: 0
55	
56	      - name: Setup Node.js
57	        uses: actions/setup-node@v4
58	        with:
59	          node-version: '22'
60	
61	      # (LOCK-REGEN) Lock regeneration below needs bun; pin the EXACT version
62	      # the build uses so the lock we commit is byte-identical to what the
63	      # frozen build install consumes.
64	      - name: Setup Bun (match the build's pinned version)
65	        uses: oven-sh/setup-bun@v2
66	        with:
67	          bun-version: '1.3.14'
68	
69	      - name: Merge upstream + resolve + gate
70	        id: do
71	        shell: bash
72	        run: |
73	          set -euo pipefail
74	          git config user.name "superset-arm64-bot"
75	          git config user.email "actions@users.noreply.github.com"
76	
77	          # --- detect the upstream tag to merge --------------------------------
78	          git remote add upstream https://github.com/superset-sh/superset.git 2>/dev/null || true
79	          # Fetch upstream's desktop-v* tags into an ISOLATED ref namespace.
80	          # Our OWN one-version release tags live in refs/tags/desktop-v<version>
81	          # (gh release create makes them at our build commit). A plain fetch into
82	          # refs/tags/ collides with every released version ("would clobber existing
83	          # tag" -> non-zero fetch -> set -e abort). The isolated namespace + --force
84	          # keeps upstream's source tags cleanly separate from our release tags.
85	          git fetch --no-recurse-submodules --force upstream \
86	            'refs/tags/desktop-v*:refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*'
87	          FORCE='${{ github.event.inputs.force_tag }}'
88	          if [ -n "$FORCE" ]; then
89	            NEWTAG="$FORCE"
90	          else
91	            LATEST=$(git for-each-ref --format='%(refname:lstrip=2)' 'refs/upstream-tags/desktop-v*' | sed -n 's/^desktop-v\([0-9][0-9.]*\)$/\1/p' | sort -V | tail -1)
92	            NEWTAG="desktop-v${LATEST}"
93	          fi
94	          BASE=$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .fork/upstream-baseline.txt)
95	          echo "baseline=$BASE  latest_upstream=$NEWTAG"
96	          if [ "$NEWTAG" = "$BASE" ]; then
97	            echo "Already at $BASE — nothing to merge."
98	            echo "should_build=false" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
99	            exit 0
100	          fi
101	          if ! git rev-parse -q --verify "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}" >/dev/null; then
102	            echo "::error::upstream tag $NEWTAG not found"; exit 1
103	          fi
104	
105	          # --- candidate branch + merge ---------------------------------------
106	          CAND="nightly/${NEWTAG}"
107	          git switch -c "$CAND"
108	          echo "Merging $NEWTAG into the fork (no auto-commit)…"
109	          set +e
110	          git merge --no-commit --no-ff "refs/upstream-tags/$NEWTAG^{commit}"
111	          set -e
112	
113	          # (WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED) .github/workflows is FORK-OWNED: the fork
114	          # carries ZERO upstream workflow files (upstream CI targets upstream's
115	          # infra/secrets and is meaningless here), and the Actions GITHUB_TOKEN
116	          # can NEVER push a .github/workflows/* change — GitHub rejects both
117	          # the candidate push and the main fast-forward with "refusing to allow
118	          # a GitHub App to create or update workflow … without `workflows`
119	          # permission" (desktop-v1.14.3 deploy-relay.yml incident: the merge,
120	          # gates and review all passed, then the push died). Restore our exact
121	          # workflow set BEFORE conflict detection: upstream-ADDED files are
122	          # dropped from index+worktree (--no-overlay), upstream edits to files
123	          # we long deleted resolve as ours, and the AI resolver never sees a
124	          # workflow path it could resurrect.
125	          git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows
126	
127	          CONFLICTS=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
128	          if [ -n "$CONFLICTS" ]; then
129	            echo "::group::conflicts to resolve"; echo "$CONFLICTS"; echo "::endgroup::"
130	            npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
131	            PROMPT="IMPORTANT: Do NOT enter plan mode. You are inside a git repository mid-merge: upstream superset tag ${NEWTAG} is being merged into our Windows-ARM64 fork. ONLY the files listed by 'git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U' are conflicted. Resolve EVERY conflicted file: combine upstream's change with our fork's feature so BOTH intents survive and our feature keeps working; where upstream deliberately removed or refactored something we had only lightly touched, follow upstream. Do NOT edit any non-conflicted file. Do NOT weaken or remove any feature marker tracked in FEATURES.md. After resolving each file run 'git add <file>'. Do NOT commit. Do NOT run any other git command. When every conflict is resolved and staged, stop. Output nothing."
132	            attempt=1
133	            while [ $attempt -le 3 ]; do
134	              echo "::group::claude resolve attempt $attempt"
135	              claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --model opus --effort high -p "$PROMPT" || true
136	              echo "::endgroup::"
137	              if [ -z "$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)" ]; then break; fi
138	              attempt=$((attempt+1))
139	            done
140	            REMAIN=$(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=U || true)
141	            if [ -n "$REMAIN" ]; then
142	              echo "::error::Opus could not resolve all conflicts for $NEWTAG — recovery: fix locally and re-baseline. Remaining:"; echo "$REMAIN"
143	              exit 1
144	            fi
145	          else
146	            echo "Clean merge — no conflicts."
147	          fi

1	# Fork feature manifest
2	
3	This file is the source of truth for **what our fork adds on top of upstream
4	superset**. The nightly upstream-merge workflow greps every marker below against
5	the merged tree; **if any marker is missing the merge is rejected** (a feature
6	was dropped or an upstream change clobbered it) and the build hard-aborts into
7	the recovery loop. Markers are deliberately distinctive identifiers that only
8	exist because of our feature code.
9	
10	When you add a new custom feature, add a marker row here in the same commit.
11	When upstream **deprecates/removes** a feature we mirrored, remove its marker row
12	in the merge that drops it (the only legitimate way a marker leaves this list).
13	
14	## Features
15	
16	| Feature | What it is | Marker |
17	|---|---|---|
18	| Native Windows ARM64 packaging | one-click NSIS installer; ARM64 node-pty/libsql/tokenizers | `npm:@lydell/node-pty`, `bun-windows-arm64` |
19	| Window controls | native `titleBarOverlay` is the sole min/max/close set on Windows | `titleBarOverlay` |
20	| Windows behaviour fixes | child-process patch, cmd.exe fallback, force-foreground, watchdog, WebGL recovery, fast startup | `windows-child-process` |
21	| Agent status dots (Claude + Codex) | per-terminal dot + workspace rollup; host-service POST + JSONL watcher | `pane-map-hook`, `StatusIndicator` |
22	| Claude Stop unhooked from notify.sh | upstream's raw Stop passthrough must never race superset-notify.py (it wiped the blue dot + yellow-hold) | `CLAUDE-STOP-UNHOOKED` |
23	| Compaction shows working | Claude context compaction (manual /compact + auto) drives the yellow dot via PreCompact / SessionStart(source=compact) | `(COMPACT-YELLOW)` |
24	| Team/workflow work shows working | background_tasks[] entry types split the post-turn dot: agent-type work (teammate/subagent/workflow) holds yellow; shell-only goes blue | `(TEAM-YELLOW)` |
25	| Codex-companion job holds the dot | detached codex worker is invisible to background_tasks[]; an active (pid-alive) codex job for the session holds yellow incl. through StopFailure AND the JSONL watcher's stream-idle/API-abort + interrupt self-heal (System 1 mirrors the same check via `codexJobActive` in agent-jsonl-watcher.ts, so a codex-only session can't false-green when the Claude API drops and no StopFailure hook fires) | `_codex_job_active` |
26	| Manual compact restores shell-blue | a manual /compact ending while only a background shell runs restores the BackgroundRunning blue (turn-end snapshot marker) instead of false-greening; the JSONL-watcher re-attach after compaction must not wipe that blue | `_shellbg_marker_path`, `(BLUE-SPECTATOR)` |
27	| ws native modules off | host-service `ws` must use pure-JS mask/unmask — a broken packaged bufferutil wedges WS receivers and kills ALL terminal keyboard input | `WS_NO_BUFFER_UTIL` |
28	| Shell-running blue dot | OSC 133 C/D command-running detection | `scanForOsc133Cd` |
29	| Non-git / multi-repo workspaces | open a non-git folder as a plain workspace | `resolveNonGitFolder` |
30	| Multi-repo branch workspaces | "Open from multi-folder" groups N git repos; "+" fans the same branch out as a worktree per repo under one container workspace | `readMultiRepoConfig`, `createMultiRepoWorkspaceFlow` |
31	| Multi-repo member management | Project Settings "Repositories" section adds/removes member repos of a multi-repo project. Add is lazy (included in NEW branch workspaces only — cleanup already tolerates a missing member worktree); remove force-removes that repo's worktrees from every existing branch container (confirm dialog; repo + branches kept) BEFORE the config rewrite so no orphan worktrees/registrations strand. Min 2 members enforced | `addMultiRepoMember`, `MultiRepoMembersSection` |
32	| Workspace branch label | the open workspace page names its branch top-right in the tab bar (click copies) — the only branch surface a non-git multi-repo container has | `WorkspaceBranchLabel` |
33	| Workspace-create failure toast | every workspace/worktree create failure raises a toast with the actual reason (e.g. a GitHub/network timeout) from the one `recordFailure` chokepoint — so a transient failure no longer reads as a silent "stuck creating → reload" | `(CREATE-FAIL-TOAST)` |
34	| Thread snooze / archive | per-thread timed Snooze + sticky Archive in the sidebar | `getWorkspaceSidebarBucket`, `APP_LAUNCH_ID`, `DashboardSidebarStateSection` |
35	| Sidebar row projection carries the fork state fields | the host-owned sidebar row projection (`rawSidebarWorkspaces` in `useDashboardSidebarData`) must carry `snoozeUntil`/`snoozeLaunchId`/`archivedAt`/`completedAt`/`deletedAt` from `v2WorkspaceLocalState` — the v1.14.2 upstream merge rebuilt that mapping and silently dropped them, which broke snooze (rows never left the active lane), emptied the Recycle Bin, vanished archived masters, and resurfaced completed threads under Archived | `(SIDEBAR-STATE-PROJECTION)` |
36	| Master rows snoozeable | `snoozeWorkspace` inserts a pre-snoozed local-state row for an auto-included main that has none yet (mirrors `archiveWorkspace`) — Snooze on a master row used to silently no-op | `(SNOOZE-MAIN)` |
37	| Sticky project removal | "Remove project from sidebar" tombstones EVERY workspace of the project incl. mains, and passive workspace route mounts (session restore, kanban split) use `placeWorkspaceFromPassiveMount`, which never re-inserts the project's sidebar row and never resurrects an existing hidden row — a removed project can only come back via an EXPLICIT open (Workspaces page, project setup/import) | `(REMOVE-STICKY)` |
38	| Case-insensitive repo-path dedupe | host-service `project.findByPath` / import-collision checks compare `projects.repoPath` case-insensitively on win32/darwin (case-insensitive filesystems) — a case-only folder rename used to miss the existing row and mint a duplicate project (cloud slug conflict suffixed `-2` instead of reusing) | `(PATH-CI-DEDUPE)` |
39	| Merge semantic review gate | every nightly upstream merge — clean or conflicted — is reviewed by Opus against this manifest BEFORE it may commit/build/publish: a cleanly-merging upstream hunk that semantically breaks a fork feature (the v1.14.2 sidebar-projection incident) is caught here, not in production. Fail closed: no parsable verdict, a BREAKAGE verdict, or a review run that modified the tree all hard-abort with the baseline untouched | `(MERGE-SEMANTIC-GATE)` |
40	| Sidebar hover-freeze | rows never re-sort while the pointer is over the project list (order applies on leave) | `(HOVER-FREEZE)` |
41	| Terminal links | plain click copies a URL/path, Ctrl/Cmd+click opens; `.html` paths open in Chrome (OS default fallback) | `useLinkClickHint`, `openHtmlInBrowser` |

Actions `GITHUB_TOKEN` can NEVER push workflow-file changes (no such scope exists for it); the `advance` main fast-forward would hit the same wall.

Fix (17cae2de9): `.github/workflows/` is fork-owned (fork carries ZERO upstream workflows — deliberate since fork creation). nightly-merge now runs `git checkout --no-overlay HEAD -- .github/workflows` right after `git merge --no-commit`, BEFORE conflict detection: upstream-added files drop from index+worktree, edits to long-deleted files resolve as ours, and the AI resolver never sees a workflow path it could resurrect. Marker `(WORKFLOW-FORK-OWNED)` rooted at `.github`. Alternatives rejected: write deploy key / PAT (new credential, plus upstream CI files would then run + fail noisily in the fork).

To adopt an upstream workflow: deliberate LOCAL commit with a user token (user tokens have `workflow` scope).
